mission.title: Install and Configure Flagger (Flux) on Kubernetes
mission.description: Flagger is a progressive delivery Kubernetes operator that automates the release process for applications. It enables canary releases, A/B testing, and blue/green deployments, reducing the risk of introducing new software versions in production.

steps.title: Add the Flagger Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Flagger Helm repository to your Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add flagger https://flagger.app
helm repo update
```

**Note:** Flagger requires a service mesh (Istio, Linkerd, App Mesh) or ingress controller (Contour, NGINX, Gloo) to be installed in your cluster. Ensure one of these is available before proceeding.

steps.title: Install Flagger using Helm
steps.description:
Install Flagger in the `flagger-system` namespace. This command will create the namespace if it does not exist.
```bash
helm install flagger flagger/flagger --namespace flagger-system --create-namespace --version 1.42.0
```

**Note:** The namespace should match your service mesh namespace (e.g., `istio-system`) or be explicitly chosen. If using Istio, consider installing in `istio-system` instead.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Flagger pods are running successfully in the `flagger-system` namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n flagger-system
```

steps.title: Configure monitoring for Flagger
steps.description:
Enable the ServiceMonitor for Prometheus to scrape Flagger metrics. Update the Helm release with the following command:
```bash
helm upgrade flagger flagger/flagger --namespace flagger-system --set serviceMonitor.enabled=true
```

steps.title: Set resource limits for Flagger
steps.description:
Set resource limits for the Flagger deployment to ensure it has sufficient resources. Update the Helm release with the following command:
```bash
helm upgrade flagger flagger/flagger --namespace flagger-system --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Flagger will show the Flagger pods running in the `flagger-system` namespace, and you will have configured monitoring and resource limits for the deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add flagger https://flagger.app
helm repo update
helm upgrade flagger flagger/flagger --namespace flagger-system --set serviceMonitor.enabled=true
helm upgrade flagger flagger/flagger --namespace flagger-system --set resources.limits.cpu=500m --set resources.limits.memory=512Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Flagger release from the `flagger-system` namespace using the following command. This will remove all associated resources except for CRDs and persistent volumes.
```bash
helm uninstall flagger --namespace flagger-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were installed with Flagger. This command will delete the CRDs from the cluster.
```bash
kubectl delete crd canaries.flagger.app
kubectl delete crd automations.flagger.app
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the `flagger-system` namespace to clean up all remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace flagger-system
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current configuration and state of the Flagger release. You can export the current values with the following command:
```bash
helm get values flagger --namespace flagger-system > flagger-backup-values.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository to fetch the latest charts and upgrade the Flagger release to the latest version. Replace `1.43.0` with the desired version if necessary.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade flagger flagger/flagger --namespace flagger-system --version 1.43.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Flagger pods are running successfully after the upgrade in the `flagger-system` namespace to ensure the upgrade was successful.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n flagger-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If Flagger pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the specific pod to diagnose the issue. Replace `<pod-name>` with the actual pod name.
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n flagger-system
```

troubleshooting.title: ServiceMonitor not scraping metrics
troubleshooting.description:
If the ServiceMonitor is not scraping metrics, verify that it is correctly configured and that Prometheus is set up to discover it. Check the ServiceMonitor status:
```bash
kubectl get servicemonitor -n flagger-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during the upgrade, check the currently installed version and ensure that the new version is compatible. You can check the installed version with:
```bash
helm list --namespace flagger-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If the Flagger pods are being OOMKilled, you may need to adjust the resource limits. Check the events for the pods to confirm this:
```bash
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n flagger-system
```

prerequisites.description: You need a running Kubernetes cluster with a service mesh (Istio, Linkerd, App Mesh) or ingress controller (Contour, NGINX, Gloo) installed, and the Helm package manager.
